title: Calendula officinalis (Calendula)
intro: Calendula officinalis (Calendula), commonly known as marigold, is a popular herb used in traditional medicine for its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. It is also widely cultivated as an ornamental plant due to its bright yellow flowers that attract pollinators like bees and butterflies. Calendula belongs to the Asteraceae family and is native to Europe, Asia, and North America. Historically, marigolds have been used for food, dye, and medicinal purposes since ancient times, with evidence dating back to Egypt and Greece.
medicine: Calendula (Calendula officinalis) has many health benefits, such as aiding digestion, reducing inflammation and promoting skin healing. It is often used to treat conditions like eczema, acne, and rashes. The plant's anti-inflammatory properties make it useful in treating muscle spasms, menstrual cramps, and headaches. Additionally, calendula has been shown to aid digestion by soothing an upset stomach and reducing bloating.

Calendula contains several medicinal constituents that contribute to its health benefits. The plant is rich in flavonoids, which are antioxidants that help protect the body against cell damage. It also contains lignans, which have been shown to reduce inflammation and promote skin healing. Additionally, calendula contains alkaloids, such as maranthocyanin, which have antibacterial properties and can be used to treat acne-prone skin.

The most commonly used parts of the calendula plant are the flowers, leaves, and stems. These parts can be consumed in various forms, including teas, tinctures, and oils. Calendula tea is often made by steeping the dried flowers or fresh leaves in hot water for several minutes. Tinctures are made by soaking the dried flowers in alcohol or vinegar. Calendula oil can be extracted from the dried petals and used topically to promote skin healing and reduce inflammation.

Although calendula is generally considered safe, there are some possible side effects that should be aware of. Some people may experience mild gastrointestinal upset, such as nausea or diarrhea, when consuming calendula tea or tinctures. Additionally, some people may experience skin irritation or allergic reactions when using calendula oil topically. It is always recommended to consult with a healthcare professional before using calendula if you have any underlying medical conditions.

To avoid possible side effects and ensure the safety of calendula use, it is important to follow some precautions. Calendula should not be used by pregnant or breastfeeding women, as there is limited information on its safety during these times. Additionally, calendula should not be used as a substitute for medical treatment for serious conditions. Always consult with a healthcare professional before using calendula if you have any underlying medical conditions or concerns.
horticulture: Calendula officinalis is an easy-to-grow annual herb that requires full sun exposure, well-drained soil, and a pH level between 6.0 to 7.5. It prefers a location with ample air circulation and direct sunlight for at least six hours per day. The plant can tolerate some shade, but it grows best in a full-sun location.

Calendula officinalis is best planted when the soil temperature is above 10°C (50°F). It can be sown directly into the ground or started indoors under bright grow lights. When planting outdoors, choose a location with well-drained soil that is slightly acidic to neutral. The seeds should be spaced about 15 cm (6 inches) apart and planted about 2 cm (1 inch) deep.

Calendula officinalis requires minimal care once it is established. It should be watered regularly, especially during dry periods. The plant prefers moist soil but not waterlogged. Fertilize every three to four weeks with a balanced fertilizer or compost tea. Deadhead the flowers as soon as they appear to encourage more blooms and prolong the growing season.

Calendula officinalis is ready to harvest in about 60 days after planting. The flowers can be picked once they have fully opened and have a bright yellow or orange center. Harvest the flowers early in the morning when the dew has evaporated. Cut the stem just below the first flower, leaving some leaves on the plant to promote more growth.

Calendula officinalis is susceptible to several pests and diseases. Aphids, whiteflies, and spider mites are common insect pests that can damage the foliage and flowers. To prevent these pests, use organic pest control methods such as insecticidal soap or neem oil. Fungal diseases such as powdery mildew and downy mildew can also affect the plant. To prevent these diseases, maintain good air circulation, ensure adequate space between plants, and avoid overhead irrigation.
botany: Calendula, with botanical name Calendula officinalis, belongs to the domain Eukarya, kingdom Plantae, phylum Magnoliophyta, class Magnoliopsida, order Asterales, family Asteraceae, genus Calendula, and species officinalis. It is commonly known as marigold or sweet alyssum.

Calendula is an annual herb that grows to a height of 30-90 cm (12-36 in). The stem is erect and hairy, while the leaves are simple, lobed, and green with yellow or orange margins. The flowers are bright yellow, have a daisy-like appearance, and are borne on fleshy stems that extend beyond the upper part of the plant. Calendula has both single and double flowers.

There are several variants of Calendula officinalis, including Calendula angustifolia (narrow-leaved marigold), Calendula aquatica (water marigold), Calendula hortensis (garden marigold), and Calendula arctica (Arctic marigold). The main difference between these variants is their leaf shape and size. Narrow-leaved marigold has narrow, lanceolate leaves, while water marigold has round, floating leaves. Garden marigold has oval-shaped leaves with jagged edges, while Arctic marigold has linear-lanceolate leaves that are grayish-green in color.

Calendula is native to southern Europe, North Africa, and western Asia but has been widely cultivated and naturalized throughout the world. It grows best in full sun and well-draining soil and prefers a slightly acidic pH of 5.5-6.0. Calendula can be found growing in gardens, fields, and along roadsides.

Calendula is a perennial herb that has a life cycle of two years or more. The first year of growth produces a rosette of leaves that forms a low shrub. The second year, the plant sends up a stem that bears flowers, followed by seeds that are dispersed by wind and animals. Calendula is self-pollinating and does not require cross-pollination to produce fruit.
